title: Batch Forward Passes: Processing Many Examples at Once
concept: A batch forward pass processes multiple training examples simultaneously in parallel using matrix operations, maximizing GPU efficiency.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the correct order of operations inside a single neuron?

options.text: Multiply inputs by weights, add bias, then apply activation function.
Apply activation function, multiply by weights, then add bias.
Add bias, apply activation function, then multiply by weights.

exercises.explain: Correct! We sum the weighted inputs plus bias, then apply the activation function.

cards.title: The post office sorting bins
cards.body: If a post office has 1,000 letters, and the mail carrier delivers them one-by-one to the destination, it takes all day. Instead, they pack 32 letters into a single crate and deliver them to the sorting machine together. Processing data in batches is how neural networks stay fast.

cards.title: Batch Processing
cards.body: Instead of running forward propagation for one row of data at a time, we group multiple rows together into a **batch** (e.g. batch size of 32 or 64). The inputs are stacked into a larger matrix, allowing the GPU to compute predictions for the entire batch in parallel.

cards.title: Taking the bus vs. individual cars
cards.body: Processing one row at a time is like 32 people driving 32 separate cars to the same office — high traffic, high overhead. Batch processing is like putting all 32 people on a single bus: they travel together, use one vehicle (one GPU calculation step), and arrive at the same time efficiently.

cards.title: Stacking the inputs
cards.body: If we predict house prices with 3 features, one house is a list: `[size, bedrooms, age]`. A batch of 32 houses is a grid with 32 rows and 3 columns. When we multiply this 32-by-3 input matrix by our 3-by-5 weight matrix, we get a 32-by-5 output matrix containing the hidden values for all 32 houses in one go.

cards.title: Batches don't mix customer data
cards.body: You might worry that stacking rows into a batch means the model mixes up different customers' data. In matrix multiplication, the rows remain completely independent. The math computes each row's prediction separately; they are simply grouped in the same step to maximize hardware speed.

cards.title: Single vs. Batch Forward Pass
cards.body: How data is fed into the model:

left.text:
One row of data processed.
Outputs one prediction.
High overhead per row.

right.text:
32 rows stacked as a matrix.
Outputs 32 predictions in parallel.
Low hardware overhead.

exercises.prompt: What does a batch size of 32 mean in a forward pass?

options.text: 32 training rows are stacked into a matrix and processed in parallel to compute 32 predictions.
The model has exactly 32 hidden layers.
The model will be retrained 32 times.

exercises.explain: Correct! Batch size determines how many training examples are processed together in parallel.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Stacking rows into a batch causes different customers' data features to mix together, corrupting individual predictions.
exercises.explain: False! Matrix math keeps rows mathematically independent; they are only grouped for parallel computation speed.

exercises.sentence: Batch processing is designed to maximize the parallel efficiency of computer chips like ___.
exercises.explain: GPUs are built for parallel matrix math, making batch processing highly efficient.

exercises.prompt: A model has a batch size of 64. If we have 640 total training rows, how many batch forward passes are needed to process all the data once?

options.text: 640, because each row is processed individually.
1, because all data is processed at once.

exercises.explain: Correct! 10 separate batches of 64 rows will cover all 640 training rows.

exercises.prompt: You are training a deep network on a large image dataset. You change your batch size from 1 to 64. What is the impact on training speed and memory usage on your GPU?

options.text: Training speed increases significantly because the GPU processes 64 images in parallel, but GPU memory usage increases because more data is held in RAM at once.
Training runs slower because 64 images take longer to upload than 1.
GPU memory usage drops to zero because of the batching structure.
There is no difference in speed or memory usage.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Batching leverages the GPU's parallel cores to speed up training, but stacking more rows requires more active memory to hold the intermediate activations. Next, let's trace a full forward pass from start to finish on a real classification task!
